name: Brugada Pattern
identifyBy: Type 1 (diagnostic): Coved ST elevation ≥2mm in ≥1 of V1–V3, followed by negative T wave - no isoelectric separation
Type 2 (suspected): Saddleback ST elevation ≥2mm, positive or biphasic T - requires provocation
Spontaneously or induced by sodium channel blockers (Ajmaline, Flecainide)
May present as syncope, nocturnal agonal breathing, or VF arrest
Pattern may be intermittent - not always present on resting ECG

measurements.qrsDuration: Normal to slightly prolonged in V1–V2
measurements.stSegment: Coved elevation ≥2mm V1–V2 with descending negative T wave

management: Urgent evacuation - risk of sudden cardiac death from VF
Withhold sodium channel blockers (Class IC antiarrhythmics, some TCAs)
Fever MUST be treated urgently - fever unmasks/exacerbates Brugada
ICD is definitive treatment for symptomatic Brugada syndrome
Quinidine may suppress arrhythmias as bridge to ICD
pearls: Fever is the most common trigger - treat aggressively with paracetamol
Brugada pattern does not equal Brugada syndrome - pattern can be drug-induced
The coved type 1 pattern is the ONLY diagnostic pattern - saddleback (Type 2) requires specialist confirmation
SCN5A gene mutation in ~20-30% - autosomal dominant inheritance
Isoproterenol can suppress electrical storm in Brugada while awaiting ICD
differentials: STEMI - anterior (V1–V3 elevation, but different morphology)
